messages.content: Despite the icon being 24x24, the button resulting from the following code is rectangular. What could be the cause?
One possible cause could be that the code is not explicitly specifying the dimensions or shape of the button.

<Button
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:layout_gravity="start"
        app:icon="@drawable/arrow_back"
        app:iconGravity="textStart"
        app:iconPadding="12dp"
        app:iconTint="@color/black"
        android:contentDescription="@string/back"
        app:backgroundTint="@android:color/white"
        />

The cause could be that the icon is not being properly constrained within the button. By default, the button's width and height are set to "wrap_content", which means they will adjust to fit the content inside. In this case, the content is the icon.

To make the button match the dimensions of the icon, you can set the width and height attributes explicitly to "24dp" or "24px" like this:

    <Button
        android:layout_width="24dp"
        android:layout_height="24dp"
        ...
        />

This will ensure that the button is a perfect square with dimensions of 24x24, matching the icon size.
I can't set the dimensions since that will stop the icon from scaling to the screen

If you want the button to scale along with the rest of the screen while preserving the aspect ratio of the icon, you can try using a combination of layout attributes and constraints to achieve that. Here's an example:

```xml
<Button
    android:layout_width="wrap_content"
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:adjustViewBounds="true"
    android:maxHeight="24dp"
    android:maxWidth="24dp"
    app:icon="@drawable/arrow_back"
    app:iconGravity="textStart"
    app:iconPadding="12dp"
    app:iconTint="@color/black"
    android:contentDescription="@string/back"
    app:backgroundTint="@android:color/white"
    />
```

In this code, `adjustViewBounds` is set to `true`, which means the button will try to adjust its bounds to preserve the aspect ratio of its content. By setting `maxHeight` and `maxWidth` to `24dp`, you specify that the button should not exceed those dimensions. This allows the button to scale with the screen while keeping the icon's aspect ratio and preventing it from becoming distorted.

Keep in mind that the final appearance of the button will depend on the surrounding layout and its constraints, so you may need to adjust those accordingly to achieve the desired result.

I apologize for the confusion. If you want to ensure that the button appears as a perfect square despite the icon being a different aspect ratio, you can use a custom drawable background for the button. Here's an example:

Create a new XML file in your drawable folder (e.g., `square_button_background.xml`) with the following code:

```xml
<shape xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android">
    <solid android:color="@android:color/white" />
    <corners android:radius="12dp" />
</shape>
```

Then, modify your button code as follows:

```xml
<Button
    android:layout_width="wrap_content"
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:layout_gravity="start"
    app:icon="@drawable/arrow_back"
    app:iconGravity="textStart"
    app:iconPadding="12dp"
    app:iconTint="@color/black"
    android:contentDescription="@string/back"
    android:background="@drawable/square_button_background"
    />
```

In this example, we set the background of the button to a custom drawable shape (`square_button_background.xml`). The `corners` attribute with a radius of `12dp` gives the button rounded corners, while the `solid` attribute sets the background color to white.

With this approach, the button will have a separate square background shape, regardless of the aspect ratio of the icon.
How do I make text in textview to be aligned to the left?

To make the text in a TextView aligned to the left, you can use the `android:gravity` attribute. Set it to `"left"` or `"start"` to align the text to the left. Here's an example:

```xml
<TextView
    android:layout_width="wrap_content"
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:gravity="left"
    android:text="Your text here"
    />
```

In this code, the `gravity` attribute is set to `"left"`, which aligns the text to the left. The actual text content can be set using the `android:text` attribute.

Alternatively, you can use `android:gravity="start"`, which automatically aligns the text to the left in left-to-right languages and to the right in right-to-left languages based on the device's current locale. This approach ensures proper text alignment in different language settings.
How do I make to elements overlap?

To make two elements overlap in Android, you can use the `FrameLayout`, `RelativeLayout`, or `ConstraintLayout` as the parent layout and adjust the positioning of the child elements within that layout.

Here's an example using `FrameLayout`:

```xml
<FrameLayout
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent">

    <!-- First element -->
    <ImageView
        android:layout_width="200dp"
        android:layout_height="200dp"
        android:src="@drawable/image1" />

    <!-- Second element -->
    <ImageView
        android:layout_width="150dp"
        android:layout_height="150dp"
        android:src="@drawable/image2" />

</FrameLayout>
```

In this example, two `ImageView` elements are added as child elements under a `FrameLayout`. The dimensions of each image view are specified using `layout_width` and `layout_height`. By default, child elements added in a `FrameLayout` are positioned at the top-left corner, allowing them to overlap.

Please note that the element added first will be placed at the bottom, and subsequent elements added will be stacked on top. You can adjust the positioning and ordering of the overlapping elements by changing the order of their declaration within the layout file.

Why isn't this button getting aligned to right? "        <Button
            android:layout_width="match_parent"
            android:layout_height="match_parent"
            android:contentDescription="@string/settings_darkmode"
            android:gravity="right"
            app:backgroundTint="@android:color/white"
            app:icon="@drawable/off"
            android:paddingVertical="11dp"
            android:paddingStart="9dp"
            android:paddingEnd="12dp"
            app:iconTint="@color/light_gray" />"

The `gravity` attribute in a `Button` affects the alignment of the button's content (text and icon) within the button itself. It does not control the alignment of the button within its parent layout.

To align the button to the right within its parent layout, you need to modify the layout attributes of the parent container. For example, you can use a `RelativeLayout` or `ConstraintLayout` and apply appropriate rules to position the button on the right side.

Here's an example using a `RelativeLayout`:

```xml
<RelativeLayout
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="match_parent">

    <Button
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:layout_alignParentRight="true"
        android:text="Button"
        />

</RelativeLayout>
```

In this example, the button is placed inside a `RelativeLayout`, and the `android:layout_alignParentRight="true"` attribute positions the button on the right side of its parent layout.

Remember to adjust the width and height of the button and apply other styling attributes as needed to achieve the desired appearance.

"    <RelativeLayout
        android:layout_width="match_parent"
        android:layout_height="wrap_content"
        android:orientation="horizontal"
        android:paddingTop="24dp">

        <Button
            android:layout_width="match_parent"
            android:layout_height="match_parent"
            android:contentDescription="@string/settings_darkmode"
            android:layout_alignParentRight="true"
            app:backgroundTint="@android:color/white"
            app:icon="@drawable/off"
            android:paddingVertical="11dp"
            android:paddingStart="9dp"
            android:paddingEnd="12dp"
            app:iconTint="@color/light_gray" />

        <TextView
            android:layout_width="match_parent"
            android:layout_height="match_parent"
            android:layout_alignParentLeft="true"
            android:text="@string/settings_darkmode"
            android:textColor="@color/black"
            android:paddingVertical="21dp"
            android:paddingStart="16dp"
            android:paddingEnd="44dp"/>





</RelativeLayout>" the button and te4xtview both overlapped and are taking all available space, despite relative layout being wrap_content

Apologies for the confusion. In a `RelativeLayout`, when both the `Button` and `TextView` have a dimension of `"match_parent"`, they will try to fill the available space within the parent layout, causing them to overlap.

To make the `Button` align to the right and the `TextView` align to the left within the `RelativeLayout`, you can set their `layout_width` attributes to `"wrap_content"` instead of `"match_parent"`.

Here's the updated code:

```xml
<RelativeLayout
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:orientation="horizontal"
    android:paddingTop="24dp">

    <Button
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="match_parent"
        android:contentDescription="@string/settings_darkmode"
        android:layout_alignParentRight="true"
        app:backgroundTint="@android:color/white"
        app:icon="@drawable/off"
        android:paddingVertical="11dp"
        android:paddingStart="9dp"
        android:paddingEnd="12dp"
        app:iconTint="@color/light_gray" />

    <TextView
        android:layout_width="wrap_content"
        android:layout_height="match_parent"
        android:text="@string/settings_darkmode"
        android:textColor="@color/black"
        android:paddingVertical="21dp"
        android:paddingStart="16dp"
        android:paddingEnd="44dp"
        android:layout_alignParentLeft="true" />

</RelativeLayout>
```

With this update, the `Button` and `TextView` will align to the right and left respectively, within the available space of the `RelativeLayout`.

The given code represents a basic activity in an Android application using Kotlin. Here's a breakdown of its functionality:

1. The `MainActivity` class extends the `AppCompatActivity` class, indicating that it's an activity in the Android application.
2. The `tag` variable is defined as a constant string with the value "TAG". It is used to identify logs in the application's logs output.
3. The `onCreate()` function is an override of the `onCreate()` method in the superclass `AppCompatActivity`. It is called when the activity is created.
4. The `setContentView(R.layout.activity_main)` sets the content view of the activity to be the layout defined in the XML file `activity_main.xml`. This XML file determines the UI layout of the activity.
5. The `onPause()` function is an override of the `onPause()` method in the superclass. It is called when the activity is pausing or moving to the background.
6. Inside the `onPause()` function, the `Log.d(tag, "Мы в методе onPause")` statement logs a debug message with the tag "TAG" and the message "Мы в методе onPause".

In summary, when the application runs and the `MainActivity` is created, it inflates the layout specified in `activity_main.xml`. When the activity is paused, it logs a debug message with the text "Мы в методе onPause".
